caseStudies.title: GPU-Accelerated Model Training
caseStudies.description: Reducing ML training cycles from days to hours.
caseStudies.overview: A computer vision startup was spending 4–6 days training production models on CPU-based infrastructure, severely limiting the team's ability to experiment and iterate. The project aimed to migrate training workloads to GPU-accelerated infrastructure and optimise the training pipeline.
caseStudies.problem: Long training cycles created a feedback loop that slowed research velocity. Researchers ran fewer experiments, favored conservative architectural choices, and struggled to meet product deadlines. Cloud GPU costs were poorly managed, often idling between jobs.
caseStudies.solution: Training infrastructure was migrated to GPU clusters with spot-instance cost optimisation. The training pipeline was refactored to support mixed-precision training, gradient accumulation, and distributed data parallelism across multiple GPUs. An experiment tracking platform was introduced to manage the increased pace of experimentation.

results.summary: Researchers now iterate at a pace previously impossible, running dozens of experiments per week. Model quality has improved as a direct result of more thorough hyperparameter exploration.

caseStudies.title: Inference Optimization at Edge
caseStudies.description: Deploying vision models on resource-constrained edge hardware.
caseStudies.overview: A manufacturing client needed to deploy quality control vision models directly on factory-floor edge devices with limited compute budgets and no reliable cloud connectivity. Models trained in the cloud needed to run efficiently on embedded GPU hardware.
caseStudies.problem: Production models required 8GB of GPU memory and 200ms inference latency, far exceeding the 2GB memory budget and 30ms latency requirement of the edge devices. Initial attempts to deploy directly resulted in out-of-memory crashes.
caseStudies.solution: Models were compressed through structured pruning, quantised to INT8 precision, and compiled using a hardware-specific inference optimiser. Knowledge distillation was used to train smaller student models that matched the accuracy of the original while fitting within the hardware constraints.

results.summary: The compressed models exceed the performance targets while maintaining near-identical accuracy. The factory now operates a fully offline quality control system with zero cloud dependency.

caseStudies.title: Synthetic Data Generation Pipeline
caseStudies.description: Augmenting scarce training data with photorealistic synthetics.
caseStudies.overview: An autonomous robotics company was bottlenecked by the cost and time required to collect and label real-world training data for its navigation models. A synthetic data pipeline was developed to generate photorealistic training scenes at scale.
caseStudies.problem: Collecting and labelling 10,000 real-world images for a new environment type took 6 weeks and cost $120,000. Rare edge cases — such as unusual lighting conditions or obstacle configurations — were nearly impossible to capture in sufficient quantities from real data collection.
caseStudies.solution: A synthetic data generation pipeline was built using a real-time 3D renderer, procedurally generating scenes with randomised lighting, textures, object placements, and camera angles. Domain randomisation ensured models trained on synthetics transferred effectively to real environments. Automatic pixel-perfect labelling eliminated manual annotation.

stats.label: Cost reduction vs real data collection

results.summary: The synthetic pipeline has fundamentally changed the economics of training data at the company. New environment types can now be supported in days rather than months, accelerating the product roadmap.
